plans.name: Plan II — Comprehensive Exam
plans.note: 48 units total. At least 16 units must be 201+ ECE courses counting toward the degree. No more than 4 units of research (299) may count as technical electives. 3.0 GPA; complete within six quarters.

plans.name: Plan I — Thesis
plans.note: 52 units total (the 4 units above the Plan II coursework are thesis / research units, e.g. ECE 299). At least 12 units must be 201+ ECE courses. No more than 8 units of research may count as technical electives. 3.0 GPA; complete within six quarters.

commonRules: All courses counted toward the degree must be taken for a letter grade and for 4 units, with the exception of research units.
Students CANNOT repeat a course unless they earned a D, F, or U grade.
Must meet the Academic Residency requirement.
Overall GPA of 3.0 or higher is required; the program must be completed within the six-quarter M.S. time limit.
Seminar courses cannot count toward the degree.

techElective.rule: Any 4-unit, 200+ course from ECE, CSE, MAE, BENG, CENG, DSC, NANO, SE, MATS, MATH, PHYS or COGS taken for a letter grade may count. Exceptions require departmental approval.
extras: Up to 12 units of upper-division ECE coursework (ECE 111+ only, excluding 195/197/198/199/210/298) OR up to two 4-unit upper-div ECE courses plus one 4-unit CSE course (CSE 100+ only, excluding 123/140/140L/143/294) may be counted.
Research that may count: ECE 299, CSE 293/298/299, MAE 299, BENG 299, NANO 299, SE 299, DSC 299 (subject to the plan's research cap).

programs.deptNotes: Standard 'Bioengineering' graduate degree (MEng / MS / PhD) — same program as base code BE75, administered under the Shu Chien-Gene Lay Department of Bioengineering. Coursework is the department's standard Bioengineering curriculum: MS = 48 units (six core courses across three Engineering + three Life Science areas, plus electives and 12 units BE 299 research; Plan I thesis or Plan II comprehensive exam); MEng = 12 courses + BENG 291 seminar (six core, three technical electives incl. BENG 295, three general electives); PhD = six core courses (three engineering, three life science) plus electives, qualifying exams and dissertation. Reuses BE75 because the required coursework is identical.

programs.name: Bioengineering with Specialization in Bioinformatics
programs.deptNotes: Bioengineering PhD PLUS the interdisciplinary Bioinformatics & Systems Biology specialization (spanning Biological Sciences, Physical Sciences, Jacobs School of Engineering, and Health Sciences). Students complete all standard Bioengineering PhD requirements (six core courses, electives, qualifying exams, dissertation) in their home department and add the bioinformatics specialization coursework; the catalog specifies no separate fixed core-course/unit count beyond the standard PhD. Intended for students working across computers, biology, medicine, and engineering. Base coursework = Bioengineering PhD (BE75).

programs.name: Bioengineering with a Specialization in Multi-Scale Biology
programs.deptNotes: Bioengineering PhD PLUS the Multi-Scale Biology specialization, spanning four schools (Biological Sciences, Physical Sciences, Jacobs School of Engineering, Health Sciences). Provides training in integrative and quantitative analysis across multiple scales of biological organization (molecule to organism, in health and disease). Students fulfill all standard Bioengineering PhD requirements plus specialization coursework; the catalog lists no separate fixed core/unit count beyond the standard PhD. Base coursework = Bioengineering PhD (BE75).

programs.name: Bioengineering with a Specialization in Quantitative Biology
programs.deptNotes: Bioengineering PhD PLUS the Quantitative Biology specialization, spanning four schools (Biological Sciences, Physical Sciences, Jacobs School of Engineering, Health Sciences). Trains students to develop and apply quantitative theoretical and experimental approaches to living systems. Specialization core = one year of theory courses plus one year of lab courses, most of which can count toward the bioengineering elective requirement. Students complete all standard Bioengineering PhD requirements. Base coursework = Bioengineering PhD (BE75).

programs.name: Bioengineering with a Specialization in Computational Neuroscience

Students fulfill all standard Bioengineering PhD academic requirements and must additionally complete a set of three core computational courses plus other coursework as directed by the Computational Neuroscience Committee, and defend a thesis on an approved topic. Base coursework = Bioengineering PhD (BE75).

plans.note: 16 core + 16 elective units of graded coursework, plus non-course milestones (ethics, rotations, seminars, TA, exams, dissertation).

programs.deptNotes: Biomedical Informatics (BMI) track of the UCSD Bioinformatics & Systems Biology PhD (jointly with DBMI). Same 32-unit coursework framework as the base BISB PhD (16 core + 16 elective units) but with a BMI-specific core and elective distribution. Core = BENG 202/CSE 282, MED 264 (replaces the BISB systems-biology core), MATH 283, plus one Fourth Core Class. Electives (16 units): min 4 units CS series + min 8 units BMI (MED) series; BMI/DBMI trainees are directed to take MED 265 and MED 267. All core/elective courses letter-graded, B or better. Also required: BNFO 294 ethics (1u), research rotations (BNFO 298) and research (BNFO 299), track colloquia/research-talk seminars, min 2 quarters TA (BNFO 500), qualifying exam (Yr2), advancement to candidacy (Yr3), dissertation defense.

programs.name: Bioinformatics and Systems Biology with a Specialization in Quantitative Biology
programs.deptNotes: Bioinformatics & Systems Biology PhD (base = BF76) combined with the interdisciplinary Quantitative Biology (qBio) specialization. Students complete the base BISB PhD coursework (32 units: 16 core + 16 elective) and, per the UCSD catalog QBIO track, direct the 16 elective units toward quantitative-biology electives. The qBio specialization (offered across Bioengineering, Biological Sciences, Biomedical Sciences, Chemistry & Biochemistry, and Physics) adds: PHYS 254 qBio seminar (~3 units over Year 1), PHYS 259A Critical Reading (~2 units, Year 1), PHYS 255 student research talks (~2 units, Years 4-5), plus 3 electives chosen from an approved qBio list — e.g. BGGN 214 (Introduction to Quantitative Biology), BGGN 212 (Quantitative Evolutionary Biology), BNFO 284 (Nonlinear Dynamics in Quantitative Biology), MAE 263 (Mechanics Inside the Cell), and PHYS 273/275/276/277/282/283 biological-physics courses. Students also attend weekly qBio seminars/events and satisfy home-department qualifying and thesis requirements. Exact unit totals for the qBio electives are not precisely published; treat as base BISB PhD plus this specialization overlay.

programs.deptNotes: MS in Computational Science, administered by the campus-wide interdisciplinary Computational Science, Mathematics and Engineering (CSME) program. Requires 38 units of coursework total, structured as: (List A) 2 numerical-methods courses = 8 units; PHYS 244 Parallel Computing = 4 units; (List A or List B) 4 additional math/methods courses = 16 units; (List C) 2 discipline-specific applied electives = 8 units; and 2 units of research/seminar. All courses except research/seminar must be letter-graded with B- or better. Students must also pass qualifying exams (final exams in three courses: two from List A plus PHYS 244, each B or above) and demonstrate basic proficiency in numerical methods/algorithms. NOTE: as of January 2022 the CSME MS program is no longer accepting new students; requirements shown are the last published curriculum.

groups.rule: One graduate course (4 units) from EACH of the three breadth areas — Theory, Systems, and Applications; letter grade, B- or higher. Full official code lists: Theory: CSE 200, 201A, 202, 203A, 203B, 205A, 207A. Systems: CSE 220, 221, 222A, 223B, 224, 231, 234, 237A-D, 240A-B, 241A, 243A, 244A. Applications: CSE 210, 216, 218, 230, 232, 250A, 251A, 251U, 252A-B, 256, 260, 280A, 284.

groups.name: Depth
groups.rule: Three graduate courses (12 units) from ONE depth area, letter grade. Depth areas: AI, Bioinformatics, Computer Engineering, Computer Systems (& Security), Database Systems, Graphics & Vision, Human-Computer Interaction, Programming Languages/Compilers/SE, Robotics, Theoretical CS. Computer Engineering majors must use Computer Engineering as depth.

groups.name: Electives and Research
groups.rule: 16 units electives/research (12 for the Interdisciplinary option, plus 12 in the secondary field). Letter grade. Max one CSE upper-division undergrad course. Max 4 units research (CSE 293). Plan I: 8–12 units CSE 298 fulfill this in place of electives.

plans.name: Plan II — Comprehensive Exam, Standard
plans.note: 12 breadth + 12 depth + 16 electives/research; pass comprehensive exams. Max 4 units research (CSE 293) count toward electives.

plans.name: Plan I — Thesis
plans.note: 12 breadth + 12 depth + 16 electives/research; requires 8–12 units CSE 298 (Independent Research) plus a thesis.

plans.name: Plan II — Comprehensive Exam, Interdisciplinary
plans.note: 48 units: 12 breadth + 12 depth + 12 CSE electives/research + 12 units approved coursework in a secondary field.

programs.deptNotes: Computer Engineering degree option within CSE. Coursework framework is identical to the Computer Science degree (CS75): MS = 40 units (breadth 12u across Theory/Systems/Applications, depth 12u, electives+research 16u; Plan I thesis or Plan II comprehensive/interdisciplinary 48u). The PhD is the single combined 'Doctor of Philosophy in Computer Science and Computer Engineering' (37-unit coursework, breadth across 4 areas, 5 electives, CSE 292 seminar, research exam). The only difference from CS75 is that Computer Engineering students must select computer engineering as their depth area; the worksheet is otherwise the same.

programs.name: Computer Science and Engineering (Advanced Manufacturing)
programs.deptNotes: MS in Computer Science and Engineering with an Advanced Manufacturing emphasis. No separate published Advanced Manufacturing curriculum could be verified on official CSE, Jacobs School MAS, or catalog pages; the Jacobs School MAS lineup lists AESE, DSE, CoSE and WES but no dedicated Advanced Manufacturing degree. Treat as the standard CSE MS structure (breadth 12u / depth 12u / electives+research 16u = 40 units, Plan I thesis or Plan II comprehensive) with a manufacturing-oriented course selection. Concrete required-course list not confirmed.

programs.name: Computer Science with Specialization in Bioinformatics
programs.deptNotes: CSE PhD (base CS75/CS76) with a specialization in Bioinformatics via the campus-wide Bioinformatics and Systems Biology (BISB) program. On top of the CSE doctoral requirements, BISB-track students take four core courses (each 4 units): Bioinformatics II (BENG 202/CSE 282, Introduction to Bioinformatics Algorithms); Bioinformatics III (BENG 203/CSE 283, Genomics, Proteomics, and Network Biology); Bioinformatics IV (MATH 283, Statistical Methods in Bioinformatics); and one Fourth Core Class chosen from CSE 280A, CSE 284, ECE 208, ECE 213, BNFO 285, or BNFO 286 (all 4u). Plus a 16-unit elective requirement (at least 4u CS-series and 4u BIO-series). Doctoral committee: min. 5 members, at least 3 (incl. chair) from BISB.

programs.name: Computer Science and Cognitive Science
programs.deptNotes: Joint doctoral / interdisciplinary PhD in Computer Science (& Engineering) and Cognitive Science. Students are admitted through the home department (CSE), complete the full CSE PhD requirements, and additionally fulfill the Cognitive Science Interdisciplinary PhD Program (IDP) requirements. Admission to the IDP is typically sought in the 2nd or 3rd year; a CogSci faculty member serves as advisor or co-advisor, with the primary advisor holding a home-department appointment. The dissertation is interdisciplinary across computation and cognition. No fixed added-course worksheet is published for CSE; requirements are arranged with the IDP.

programs.name: Computer Science and Engineering with a Specialization in Computational Science
programs.deptNotes: CSE PhD with a specialization in Computational Science via the campus-wide Computational Science, Mathematics and Engineering (CSME) program. Home department is CSE; students complete all CSE PhD requirements plus the CSME specialization. CSME adds: pass final exams in three qualifying courses — MATH 275 or MAE 290B (Numerical PDE), PHYS 244 or CSE 260 (Parallel Computing), and one List A core course; and two List B elective courses, both outside the home department (first for a letter grade, second may be pass/fail). Students must demonstrate advanced-undergraduate proficiency in numerical analysis and in algorithms/data structures by end of first year. Dissertation must be interdisciplinary and involve computational science.

programs.name: ECE (Advanced Manufacturing)
programs.deptNotes: Cataloged as 'Electrical and Computer Engineering (Advanced Manufacturing) MS' — the standard ECE MS pursued through the interdepartmental curriculum in advanced manufacturing (offered alongside the applied-ocean-sciences and materials-science interdepartmental curricula). It follows the ECE MS structure, not a separate professional worksheet: Plan I (thesis) = 52 units total; Plan II (comprehensive exam) = 48 units total; both within a 6-quarter limit. A minimum of 12 units (Plan I) / 16 units (Plan II) must be 201+ level ECE graduate courses counting toward the degree. Plan I ends in a written thesis + oral defense; Plan II requires passing three course-hosted comprehensive exams. No distinct fixed advanced-manufacturing course list is published beyond the standard ECE MS requirements plus advanced-manufacturing-area coursework selected via the ECE degree planner, so no fabricated worksheet is provided.

programs.name: Electrical Engineering (Electronic Circuits and Systems) and Cognitive Science
programs.deptNotes: Joint doctoral title 'Electrical Engineering (Electronic Circuits and Systems) and Cognitive Science' — the ECE PhD in the Electronic Circuits & Systems research area (base worksheet EC78) taken jointly with the Cognitive Science Interdisciplinary PhD Program (IDP). The student completes the full ECE (ECS) doctoral requirements PLUS the added Cognitive Science IDP requirements. Added CogSci coursework per the catalog: two of the three foundation courses — COGS 201 (Neural Dynamics of Cognition), COGS 202 (Cognitive Science Foundations: Computational Modeling of Cognition), COGS 203 (Cognitive Science Foundations: Theories and Methods in the Study of Cognitive Phenomena); plus COGS 200 (Cognitive Science Seminar) for three quarters (one quarter may be satisfied by another similar seminar such as COGS 260); plus an individualized secondary-specialization plan developed with advisers. The main UCSD catalog degrees-offered list does not itemize this specific EE+CogSci joint pairing, so confidence is capped at medium. This is the base ECS doctoral program plus the interdisciplinary CogSci specialization, not a separate worksheet.

programs.name: Engineering Sciences (Electrical and Computer Engineering)
programs.deptNotes: Cataloged as 'Engineering Sciences (Electrical and Computer Engineering) (Joint with San Diego State University) PhD' — this is the SDSU/UCSD Joint Doctoral Program, a doctoral degree in Engineering Science (Electrical and Computer) awarded jointly by the Regents of the University of California and the Trustees of the California State University. Coursework is essentially the same as the standard UCSD ECE doctoral coursework: total course requirements equal the MS degree — forty-eight units (twelve quarter courses), of which at least thirty-six units must be graduate courses, normally completed within two years of full-time study; students holding an MS in EE may substitute prior graduate courses with advisor approval. It is an administrative/joint doctoral title rather than a distinct course-list worksheet, so it mirrors the ECE PhD coursework.

programs.name: Engineering Sciences (Engineering Physics)
programs.deptNotes: Engineering Sciences with a specialization in Engineering Physics is one of the MAE graduate specializations (MS and PhD track; CPH = advancement to PhD candidacy). MS = 36 units: Plan I (thesis) is 24 units (6 courses) of coursework + 12 units MAE 299 research; Plan II (comprehensive exam) is 36 units (9 courses) plus passing three comprehensive-exam components within the specialization. Engineering Physics is not among the specializations with a course table on the current maeweb MS page, so no specialization-specific course list could be verified.

programs.name: Materials Science and Engineering with a Specialization in Multi-Scale Biology
programs.deptNotes: Base degree = Materials Science and Engineering PhD (MS76); students complete all MSE doctoral requirements PLUS the interdisciplinary Specialization in Multi-Scale Biology, administered by the UCSD Interfaces Graduate Training Program. Added specialization requirements: complete the core course BENG 219 'Data Science for Multiscale Biology', PLUS at least two of eight hands-on interdisciplinary graduate laboratory courses (minimum three specialization courses total). Students apply in the Spring quarter of their first or second year via a brief research-interest summary. Completing home-department + specialization requirements yields a 'PhD in Materials Science and Engineering with a Specialization in Multi-Scale Biology'. MSE is one of nine participating home departments. Specialization course numbers/titles beyond BENG 219 are not enumerated on the fetched pages, so a full worksheet is not fabricated.

programs.name: Chemical Engineering with a Specialization in Multiscale Biology
programs.deptNotes: PhD in Chemical Engineering WITH a Specialization in Multi-Scale Biology. Coursework = the standard Chemical Engineering PhD (base code CE75, already worksheeted) PLUS the interdisciplinary Multi-Scale Biology specialization administered by the UCSD Interfaces Graduate Training Program (spanning Jacobs School of Engineering, Health Sciences, Biological Sciences, Physical Sciences). Added specialization coursework: complete a minimum of THREE courses total = required BENG 219 (Data Science for Multiscale Biology) PLUS at least two hands-on graduate laboratory courses chosen from the Interfaces list, e.g. BENG 216 (Neuromorphic Integrated Bioelectronics), BENG 277 (Tissue Engineering), BENG/RAD 278 (Medical Imaging Laboratory), BENG 235 (Molecular Imaging and Quantitation in Living Cells), BENG/CHEM/MATH/PHARM 276 (Numerical Analysis for Multiscale Biology), BENG/CHEM 283 (Supramolecular Structure Determination Laboratory), BENG/BGGN 260/PHYS 279 (Multiscale Neurodynamics). Students must satisfy both their home ChemE PhD requirements and the Interfaces specialization requirements. Per-course unit counts not published on the fetched pages, so no full worksheet fabricated.

programs.name: NanoEngineering with a Specialization in Multiscale Biology
programs.deptNotes: PhD in NanoEngineering WITH a Specialization in Multi-Scale Biology. Coursework = the standard NanoEngineering PhD (base code NA75, already worksheeted) PLUS the interdisciplinary Multi-Scale Biology specialization run by the UCSD Interfaces Graduate Training Program (four schools: Jacobs School of Engineering, Health Sciences, Biological Sciences, Physical Sciences). Added specialization coursework: complete a minimum of THREE courses total = required BENG 219 (Data Science for Multiscale Biology) PLUS at least two hands-on graduate laboratory courses from the Interfaces list, e.g. BENG 216 (Neuromorphic Integrated Bioelectronics), BENG 277 (Tissue Engineering), BENG/RAD 278 (Medical Imaging Laboratory), BENG 235 (Molecular Imaging and Quantitation in Living Cells), BENG/CHEM/MATH/PHARM 276 (Numerical Analysis for Multiscale Biology), BENG/CHEM 283 (Supramolecular Structure Determination Laboratory), BENG/BGGN 260/PHYS 279 (Multiscale Neurodynamics). Students satisfy both their home NanoEngineering PhD requirements and the Interfaces specialization requirements. Per-course unit counts not published on the fetched pages, so no full worksheet fabricated.

programs.deptNotes: Specialization/focus area within the base MS in Structural Engineering (SE75; 48 quarter units, 12 courses, Thesis or Comprehensive Exam plan). The department does not publish a standalone worksheet for SHM/Prognosis/Validated Simulations as a separate MS code; it is a multi-disciplinary focus integrating structural engineering, ECE, and CSE for data acquisition/sensing, signal processing, and predictive modeling. The catalog advises students in this area to take SHM focus-area courses plus outside-SE electives MAE 283, MAE 261, ECE 251AN, ECE 251BN, ECE 254, and CSE 291 to satisfy the outside-structural-engineering requirement. For the Comprehensive option the 4-unit independent study SE 296 must be completed as a capstone project integrating SHM/prognosis/NDE technology areas. Same base MS structure (2 focus sequences, SE 290 seminar) otherwise.

programs.deptNotes: Administrative title variant. 'Engineering Sciences (Structural Engineering)' is the formal Jacobs School of Engineering doctoral degree title used for the PhD in Structural Engineering; the departmental requirements are identical to the base Structural Engineering doctoral program. The PhD is exam/research based (no fixed course worksheet): Department Qualifying Exam covering four focus areas (with one minor focus outside SE), Advancement to Candidacy Senate Exam (committee of 4+), 3 quarters of SE 290 before the DQE, a teaching/mentorship requirement, and a dissertation final defense (min. 3 quarters after candidacy). Coursework/requirements are the same as the base Structural Engineering graduate program (SE75).

programs.name: Structural Engineering with a Specialization in Computational Science
programs.deptNotes: Base Structural Engineering PhD PLUS the interdisciplinary Specialization in Computational Science (CSME doctoral specialization). All standard SE PhD requirements apply (DQE, Advancement to Candidacy exam, teaching requirement, dissertation defense). ADDED specialization requirements: (1) pass three qualifying exams — MATH 275 or MAE 290B (numerical PDEs); PHYS 244 or CSE 260 (parallel computing); and one course from approved List A (e.g. MATH 270A-C, MATH 271A-C, MATH 272A-C, MAE 223, MAE 232/SE 276A-C, PHYS 221A-B, SE 233) — all passed by end of year 2 (year 3 with petition). (2) Three elective courses (4 units each) from List B (any List A course, PHYS 241-242, MAE 222, MAE 261, SE 277, SE 278A-B, CHEM 215, BGGN 260, ECE 272, CSE 250A-B, MATH 210A-C), with a minimum of two electives taken outside the home department; one may be Pass/Fail, the other letter grade. (3) Computer engineering proficiency demonstrated by end of first year.
